name: Early Winter Couloir
watch_out: Three large chockstones requiring dry tooling and technical mixed climbing skills; ice coating on rocks creates variable grading
Significant cornice at top requiring careful navigation with multiple bypass options; cornice collapse risk during warm spells
Steep snow pitches 40-65 degrees throughout; loose snow and slough avalanches on gully walls, particularly during spring warming
Limited seasonal window (April-early May after pass opens); narrow timeframe when route is in condition before rapid snowmelt
Mixed terrain transitions between ice, snow, and rock climbing; protection limited on rock sections
Moderate exposure on approach and ascent; falls could be fatal on steeper pitches

name: East Face Variation
watch_out: Significant avalanche danger on approach; avoid summer trail during winter (high hazard zone); use east face approach to Source Lake
Steep slab climbing with mixed grade M4-5 sections; requires solid mixed climbing ability on exposed terrain
Second pitch features long run-outs on thin ice with difficult screw/picket protection; high consequence if placements fail
Balancy mixed terrain over exposed east face; slipping on slab sections over significant drops
Rime ice buildup on exposed pitches; weather deterioration common in Snoqualmie Pass area
Corniced terrain on descent; rappel hazards with potential rime/ice accumulation on anchor points

name: NE Couloir
watch_out: Spring avalanche hazard on steep snow couloir (50+ degree slope angle); early season ascents have highest risk during rapid warming cycles
Early seasonal snowmelt limits climbing window to weeks after snow access opens; rapid transitions between stable and unstable snow conditions
Remote alpine cirque location with limited escape options; terrain trap if avalanche occurs; 45-minute approach from trailhead
650-foot vertical exposure through narrow couloir; rockfall and icefall hazard from cliffs above; 750-foot near-vertical rock walls above main couloir
Highly variable conditions dependent on seasonal snowpack; reconnaissance needed to assess current slide activity before committing

name: New York Gully
watch_out: Steep northwest-facing gully avalanche terrain; entire route on avalanche-prone NW face; proximity to Phantom Slide zone adds risk
Highly variable ice coverage; recent reports indicate route can be completely dry with minimal ice requiring only 1-2 screw placements total
Complex approach through thick tree coverage and steep snowed-up cliff bands; navigation difficulty without prior knowledge or established skin track
WI3-WI4+ ice climbing with mixed A1-2 aid sections; sustained steep terrain with potential icefall from ice bulges
Steep snowed-up cliffs on NW face create objective hazard from falling ice and cornices; wind-loaded terrain on N and W aspects
Route finding challenges in poor visibility; 4-6 pitch climb with potential for becoming off-route in whiteout conditions

name: North Face
watch_out: Poor rock quality on winter ascent makes rock sections dangerous; wet and icy rock creates slick conditions and unreliable protection
Multiple fatal fall hazards identified in trip reports; unprotected sections and exposure over steep terrain; fall consequences are serious
Significant icefall hazard from sun exposure; sun hits gully creating dangerous ice fall conditions (ice chunks descending mid-climb)
Blue ice with wet snow accumulation creating poor foot placement; sections near vertical requiring dual-tool technique
Short seasonal window due to low elevation (5,700 ft) and west-facing aspects; very limited periods with stable conditions
12-pitch alpine route with complex rappel descent; icefall risk during descent in daylight-constrained winter season
Avalanche terrain throughout; slopes suitable for slab formation (30-45 degree gully angles below near-vertical sections)

name: Northeast Buttress
watch_out: Highly conditions-dependent difficulty; mixed climbing (M4) pitches vary from easy snow/ice to sustained steep sections based on current conditions
Steep ice bulge (A2-3 rating) on pitch 3 with notoriously difficult anchor placement; anchors unreliable and sparingly available above pitch 3
Heavily corniced rappel descent; cornice collapse risk during warm spells; potential for rappel line being cut by cornices
S-shaped gully on pitch 1 with variable mixed climbing up to M4; conditions-dependent route difficulty affects protection options
Long pitch to base of ice bulge (70-80m) on easy terrain, but terrain becomes avalanche-prone on wider snow slopes
Exposed approach traverse; wind-loaded terrain near Snoqualmie Pass; rapid weather changes common
Multiple rappel anchors required for descent; deteriorating conditions during descent can create urgent retreat scenarios

name: Northeast Slabs
watch_out: Significant avalanche danger on approach to The Tooth; avoid summer path entirely due to high avalanche exposure
Second pitch protection difficulties; long run-outs on thin ice with inadequate screw/picket placement options; high consequences for protection failure
Balancy mixed slab climbing requiring precise footwork; exposed low 5th class/M3 pitch over east face; 50-70 degree ice sections
Early season ascents face steeper avalanche risk on approach gully; spring warming creates slough and slab hazards
Rime ice accumulation on exposed upper pitches reduces friction and increases slipping risk; direct north aspect can produce rime ice in winter storms
Weather exposure on approach valley; compressed daylight window for winter climbs increases risk of descent in darkness

name: SW Couloir and Face
watch_out: Two large transverse crevasses on lower couloir crossed via snow bridges; bridges can collapse or weaken with daily warming cycles
Icefall hazard in lower Curtis Glacier section (600' steep snow 45-70 degrees); significant objective danger from serac and ice chunks on both sides
60-foot section of unprotected 5.0 rock climbing; minimal safety options if climber falls; highly exposed section
Upper Curtis icefall hazard in low visibility; disorientation can lead climbers directly into falling ice zone; critical to stay high and traverse directly below Hourglass
Steep ice sections (60-70 degrees at bottom, 70+ degrees at top) requiring sustained WI3 climbing; ice bulges may be 80+ degrees
Complex route finding on multi-glacier system; summit area routefinding difficult; multiple descent options with different hazard profiles
Grade III terrain with 3300' vertical; sustained elevation and exposure; weather changes create dangerous conditions quickly on open glaciers
